accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 carves out confidentiality breaches from liability cap (unlimited exposure) + Section 7.1 defines Customer Data as Lattice's Confidential Information + Section 5.3 Security Incident notification obligation with 72-hour window + Section 8.2 disclaimer 'DOES NOT WARRANT THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The interaction of four provisions creates existential unlimited liability that no individual specialist would flag in isolation. Any data breach triggers both Security Incident reporting and unlimited confidentiality-breach liability, while the security disclaimer means Lattice has no contractual obligation to prevent the breach. Customer Data exposure becomes an unlimited-liability event with no corresponding security obligation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The security disclaimer + unlimited confidentiality liability + Customer Data definition creates a structure where Lattice has unlimited exposure for an event it has disclaimed any obligation to prevent. The E&O/cyber insurance ($5M) is nine figures short of typical data-breach exposure in class actions. This is an existential hazard for both parties that emerges only from reading all four sections together.
accepted_findings.position: Either (1) cap confidentiality liability at 3x-5x the general cap, OR (2) remove the security disclaimer from Section 8.2 and make security obligations expressly warranted, OR (3) narrow Section 7.1 so that Customer Data is Customer's Confidential Information (not Lattice's), eliminating the unlimited-liability trigger on breach.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept 5x super-cap for confidentiality breach AND remove security/data-loss disclaimer from Section 8.2, creating bounded liability with enforceable security obligations.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Unlimited confidentiality liability + security disclaimer + Customer Data defined as Lattice Confidential Information. This structure is uninsurable and creates bankruptcy risk on first incident.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Articulates a specific structural risk created by the interplay of four contract provisions, quantifies the insurance gap against real-world exposure, characterizes it as existential, and frames it as a mutual hazard requiring deal-level resolution.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b) payment acceleration (Customer pays 100% of remaining contract value on termination) + Section 4.2 auto-renewal with 60-day notice requirement + Section 3.5 uncapped price increases at each renewal + Section 4.3 asymmetric 30/60-day cure periods
accepted_findings.external_comment: The termination and renewal provisions combine to create a compounding lock-in with escalating exit costs. Customer faces: auto-renewal every 12 months unless 60-day advance notice given; uncapped price increases at each renewal; 100% payment of remaining contract value if Customer terminates for convenience at any point; and only 30 days to cure breaches before Lattice can terminate and trigger the payment acceleration, versus 60 days for Customer to terminate Lattice.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer who misses one 60-day non-renewal notice is locked into another year at whatever price Lattice sets, and if Customer attempts to exit mid-term, Customer owes 100% of the inflated renewal fees for the full year. Over a multi-year relationship with compounding renewals, this creates exponential lock-in. The existential element is the compounding effect: not just that exit is expensive, but that the expense escalates automatically and Customer has no mechanism to cap it short of perfect calendar discipline on 60-day notices.
accepted_findings.position: Require ALL of: (1) cap on payment acceleration (50% with 6-month ceiling); (2) cap on price increases (5% or CPI); (3) mutual 60-day cure periods; (4) termination-for-convenience right with capped fee after Year 1.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept payment acceleration at 75% with 12-month cap AND price increase cap at 7% or CPI AND mutual 60-day cure. This breaks the compounding ratchet by capping both the exit cost and the annual escalation.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any structure that combines uncapped price increases + 100% payment acceleration + auto-renewal. Customer cannot budget or exit under this structure, making the relationship existentially unmanageable.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific interlocking mechanics of three provisions in this contract (auto-renewal + uncapped price increases + 100% acceleration), demonstrates how they compound over time, and identifies the specific failure mode (missing the 60-day notice triggers escalating costs), though stops short of deal-specific leverage analysis or action weighting.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees accrued through the effective date of termination
accepted_findings.external_comment: We need to align the termination payment obligation with actual service delivery. Under the current language, Customer would owe full subscription fees for periods when no service is being provided, which creates an economic penalty unrelated to Lattice's actual damages.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As drafted, Customer cannot exit the relationship without paying 100% of remaining contract value regardless of service need, business change, or vendor performance short of material breach. This eliminates Customer's ability to respond to changed circumstances and creates liability potentially in the millions for a multi-year subscription, functionally eliminating Customer's termination right.
accepted_findings.position: Customer pays only for services actually received through the termination date. No payment acceleration for undelivered services.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer pays a early-termination fee equal to three months of recurring fees, capped at the lesser of that amount or the remaining contract value.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any payment acceleration exceeding 50% of remaining contract value for Customer-initiated convenience termination.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees accrued through the effective date of termination
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's payment acceleration structure, quantifies the risk relative to contract duration (multi-year subscription, potentially millions), and connects the clause to Customer's specific role and how it undermines the broader termination framework.

accepted_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), AND (C) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD, IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY. NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, EACH PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY FOR BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY) SHALL NOT EXCEED THREE TIMES THE AMOUNT OF THE GENERAL LIABILITY CAP SET FORTH IN THE IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING SENTENCE.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 8.6 currently carves confidentiality breaches out of the liability cap entirely, exposing both parties to unlimited liability for any breach of the confidentiality obligations. Given the breadth of information deemed Confidential Information, including the terms of this Agreement and all Customer Data, this creates disproportionate exposure. A super-cap tied to a multiple of the general cap is industry-standard for confidentiality claims and aligns remedies with the likelihood and magnitude of actual harm.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Unlimited liability for confidentiality breach means any disclosure of Customer Data exposes Lattice to damages exceeding the total contract value by orders of magnitude. Section 7.1 defines Customer Data as Lattice Confidential Information; any data-breach triggers both Security Incident notice and unlimited-liability confidentiality breach. With no cap, a single incident in a $100K annual contract could generate $10M+ liability. The E&O insurance ($5M) does not cover this gap in many cyber policies.
accepted_findings.position: 3x super-cap for confidentiality breach; carve-out limited to gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud.
accepted_findings.fallback: 5x super-cap for confidentiality breach; carve-out includes ordinary negligence if Customer demonstrates actual damages exceeding the super-cap.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Unlimited confidentiality liability with broad Confidential Information definition and no fault threshold.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), AND (C) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD, IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY. NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, EACH PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY FOR BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY) SHALL NOT EXCEED THREE TIMES THE AMOUNT OF THE GENERAL LIABILITY CAP SET FORTH IN THE IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING SENTENCE.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract provisions (Section 7.1, Security Incident notice), calculates concrete exposure based on contract value ($100K → $10M+ liability), and identifies insurance gap against specific policy limits ($5M E&O), though stops short of explicit deal-posture trade-off analysis.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay (i) all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination, and (ii) a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, provided that such termination fee shall not exceed the Fees for six (6) months of Subscription Services
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 4.4(b) requires Customer to pay 100% of remaining contract value if Customer terminates for any reason other than Lattice's breach, even if Customer terminates due to business necessity, budget constraints, or other legitimate reasons. This effectively eliminates Customer's practical ability to exit the relationship. We propose a capped early-termination fee that balances Lattice's revenue expectations with Customer's need for flexibility in changed circumstances.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Requiring full payment of the entire remaining term creates a lock-in effect: Customer cannot exit even if the Subscription Services become unsuitable for Customer's needs, Customer's business changes direction, or Customer experiences financial hardship. In a multi-year SaaS contract, this could mean paying hundreds of thousands of dollars for services Customer cannot use. This is particularly harsh given the auto-renewal provision in Section 4.2, which could lock Customer into additional years if the non-renewal notice is missed.
accepted_findings.position: Early termination fee of 50% of remaining contract value, capped at six months of Fees.
accepted_findings.fallback: Early termination fee of 75% of remaining contract value, capped at twelve months of Fees, with no termination fee if Customer terminates after the first anniversary of the Initial Term.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Full payment of remaining contract value with no cap and no sunset. Customer must have some exit path short of Lattice's material breach.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay (i) all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination, and (ii) a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, provided that such termination fee shall not exceed the Fees for six (6) months of Subscription Services
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract provisions (references Section 4.2's auto-renewal mechanism and connects it to the termination fee), analyzes the compounding risk of these provisions together, and grounds the concern in this contract's multi-year structure rather than just generic SaaS principles.

accepted_findings.source_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
accepted_findings.proposed_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate; and (c) cyber liability insurance, which must include coverage for network security and privacy liability, with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
accepted_findings.external_comment: Given that the Subscription Services involve hosting and processing Customer Data, dedicated cyber liability coverage is essential. The current optional language does not provide sufficient assurance that cyber risks—network security failures, data breaches, privacy violations—are adequately covered.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Cyber incidents involving Customer Data create direct liability for Customer under data protection laws and customer contracts. Without required cyber coverage, Customer has no assurance that Lattice's E&O policy includes cyber perils or sufficient limits to address third-party claims arising from a breach, leaving Customer exposed to uninsured indemnity obligations.
accepted_findings.position: Require standalone cyber liability coverage with $5M limits covering network security, privacy liability, and data breach response costs. Separate from E&O policy to ensure dedicated coverage for data-related incidents.
accepted_findings.fallback: Require that the E&O policy expressly include cyber liability coverage as a mandatory component with separate sublimit of at least $5M for cyber claims, evidenced by endorsement.
accepted_findings.walkaway: No requirement for any form of cyber coverage when contract involves processing Customer Data.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate; and (c) cyber liability insurance, which must include coverage for network security and privacy liability, with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with the specific contract structure by connecting the E&O policy language to the Customer's actual exposure under indemnity obligations and data protection laws, explaining why the current provision creates uninsured risk rather than merely citing a playbook requirement.

accepted_findings.category: SLA remedy framing creates unintended liability cap interaction
accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The 'sole and exclusive remedy' framing for SLA failures creates ambiguity when read alongside the general liability cap in Section 8.6. If service credits are uncapped but also the 'sole remedy,' and the general cap does not explicitly carve out SLA credits, a court could construe the two provisions as internally contradictory. Customer requires either: explicit confirmation that SLA credits do not count against the Section 8.6 liability cap, or an express cap on total SLA credits in any twelve-month period, with clarity that such cap is separate from and additional to the Section 8.6 cap.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If SLA credits are uncapped and Provider's general liability is capped at twelve months of fees under Section 8.6, Customer may be unable to recover aggregate SLA credits exceeding that amount in a sustained outage scenario, defeating the purpose of the SLA. Alternatively, if credits are deemed outside the cap but uncapped themselves, Provider faces theoretically unlimited exposure through the SLA-credit mechanism despite an otherwise-comprehensive liability limitation.
accepted_findings.position: Add new sentence to Section 2.3: 'For the avoidance of doubt, service credits payable under Exhibit A are subject to a cumulative annual cap equal to [25%] of the Fees paid or payable under the applicable Order Form in the twelve (12) months preceding the date the credits accrued, and such credits shall not count against the liability cap set forth in Section 8.6.'
accepted_findings.fallback: Add language to Section 8.6 explicitly stating whether SLA credits do or do not count against the general cap, and if they do not, impose a reasonable annual cap on total SLA credits (industry standard is 10-25% of annual subscription fees).
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's liability cap structure in Section 8.6 and analyzes the interaction between uncapped SLA credits and the twelve-month fee cap, showing reasoning grounded in how these specific provisions work together.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 9.1 permits unlimited subcontracting without notice or consent + Section 5.2 information security obligations + Section 9.2 insurance requirements do not flow down to subcontractors + Section 8.3 IP indemnity carve-outs include 'use in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Lattice'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 9.1 allows Lattice to subcontract any obligation, including Customer Data processing, to any third party without notice to Customer. Section 5.2 obligates Lattice to maintain security safeguards for Customer Data. Section 9.2 requires Lattice to maintain insurance, but contains no flow-down requirement for subcontractors. Section 8.3 carves out IP indemnity if the infringement arises from 'use in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Lattice.' Result: Lattice can route Customer Data through subcontractors with no security vetting visibility to Customer, no insurance requirement on the subcontractor, and if the subcontractor's platform infringes IP, Section 8.3 may eliminate Lattice's indemnity obligation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer loses security visibility (no notice under Section 9.1), loses insurance backstop (no subcontractor insurance requirement under Section 9.2), and may lose indemnity coverage (Section 8.3 carve-out for non-Lattice products). For a SaaS platform relying on third-party infrastructure (cloud hosting, CDN, payment processors), this is a material gap.
accepted_findings.position: Require: (1) advance written notice of subcontractors processing Customer Data, with right to object to subcontractors that do not meet equivalent security standards; (2) flow-down of insurance requirements to subcontractors handling Customer Data or providing core platform functionality; (3) clarification that Section 8.3 indemnity applies to infringement arising from Lattice-authorized subcontractors.
accepted_findings.fallback: Require annual list of material subcontractors with right to audit subcontractor security upon reasonable notice, and clarify that 'Subscription Services' in Section 8.3 includes subcontractor-provided components authorized by Lattice.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract sections (9.1, 9.2, 8.3) and shows how their interaction creates compounded risk for a SaaS platform with third-party dependencies, though stops short of deal-specific leverage analysis or action recommendation.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 2.6 Service Modifications and Discontinuation. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ninety (90) days' advance written notice before discontinuing any material feature or functionality of the Subscription Services, or before making any modification that materially diminishes the core functionality described in the applicable Order Form. If Lattice discontinues a material feature or functionality and cannot provide a commercially reasonable substitute, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form upon thirty (30) days' written notice and receive a pro-rated refund of prepaid Fees for the terminated portion of the Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: No specialist flagged the absence of service-discontinuation or material-modification obligations. Section 2.4 permits Lattice to 'modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time' with only a prohibition on modifications that 'materially diminish the core functionality... during the then-current Subscription Term.' This language permits Lattice to discontinue features entirely at renewal, or to make breaking changes mid-term as long as Lattice characterizes them as not materially diminishing core functionality. For a customer relying on specific features for business operations, this creates a risk that the service Customer contracted for is unilaterally changed or eliminated with no notice, no refund, and no exit right.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer pays annually in advance for a service that can be materially changed or discontinued; the subscription model creates dependency—Customer integrates the platform into operations and cannot easily switch; SaaS providers routinely deprecate features, sunset APIs, and force migrations to new versions, often with inadequate notice. Without a contractual notice and exit-right provision, Customer has no remedy when critical features are discontinued mid-term or at renewal.
accepted_findings.position: Ninety-day advance notice for material feature discontinuation or modification, with termination right and pro-rated refund if no commercially reasonable substitute is provided.
accepted_findings.fallback: Sixty-day advance notice, with termination right and refund only if the discontinued feature was explicitly identified in the Order Form as core functionality.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 2.6 Service Modifications and Discontinuation. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ninety (90) days' advance written notice before discontinuing any material feature or functionality of the Subscription Services, or before making any modification that materially diminishes the core functionality described in the applicable Order Form. If Lattice discontinues a material feature or functionality and cannot provide a commercially reasonable substitute, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form upon thirty (30) days' written notice and receive a pro-rated refund of prepaid Fees for the terminated portion of the Subscription Term.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References standard SaaS concerns (feature deprecation, API sunset, vendor lock-in) and general subscription economics, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the client's leverage position, or other protective provisions that may exist in the document.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.6 Invoice Disputes. Customer may dispute any invoice item in good faith by providing written notice to Lattice within thirty (30) days of receipt of the applicable invoice, specifying the disputed amount and the basis for the dispute. Customer may withhold payment of the specific disputed amount only, provided that Customer pays all undisputed amounts on the original due date. The Parties will work in good faith to resolve any dispute within fifteen (15) business days. If the Parties cannot resolve the dispute, either Party may pursue available remedies under this Agreement. No late fees or interest will accrue on any disputed amount during the pendency of a good-faith dispute.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We would like to include a standard invoice-dispute mechanism that protects both parties' cash flow while allowing good-faith challenges to billing errors without triggering suspension or late fees.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without dispute-withholding rights, Customer faces suspension and accumulating late fees if it withholds payment on even a clearly erroneous invoice line item. This forces Customer to pay first and seek recovery later, reversing the economic leverage and requiring Customer to fund Lattice's cash flow even when invoicing is wrong.
accepted_findings.position: Customer may withhold disputed amounts with written notice of basis; no late fees or suspension during good-faith dispute; 30-day notice window to raise disputes.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer may withhold disputed amounts exceeding $10,000; must pay amounts under that threshold and seek refund; late fees and suspension tolled during dispute for withheld amounts only.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.6 Invoice Disputes. Customer may dispute any invoice item in good faith by providing written notice to Lattice within thirty (30) days of receipt of the applicable invoice, specifying the disputed amount and the basis for the dispute. Customer may withhold payment of the specific disputed amount only, provided that Customer pays all undisputed amounts on the original due date. The Parties will work in good faith to resolve any dispute within fifteen (15) business days. If the Parties cannot resolve the dispute, either Party may pursue available remedies under this Agreement. No late fees or interest will accrue on any disputed amount during the pendency of a good-faith dispute.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's payment mechanics, analyzes how the absence of this provision reverses economic leverage in disputes, and shows how the current structure (suspension/late fees) forces the client into a disadvantageous position under this agreement's framework.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.7 Audit Rights. No more than once per calendar year, upon thirty (30) days' prior written notice, Customer may audit or cause an independent third-party auditor to audit Lattice's books and records relating to the calculation of Fees, usage metrics, and service-level performance under this Agreement. Such audit will be conducted during Lattice's normal business hours and in a manner that does not unreasonably interfere with Lattice's operations. The auditor must be bound by confidentiality obligations no less protective than those in Section 7. Customer will bear the cost of the audit unless the audit reveals an overpayment of more than five percent (5%) for the audited period, in which case Lattice will reimburse Customer for the reasonable cost of the audit and refund the overpayment within thirty (30) days.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We would like to include a standard audit right to verify billing accuracy and service-level compliance, particularly given the annual advance invoicing structure.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer pays annually in advance with no ability to verify that usage-based fees, user-count charges, or service-level credits are calculated correctly. Annual invoices on a multi-user SaaS platform can reach hundreds of thousands of dollars; a 5% billing error at that scale is material, and Customer has no mechanism to detect or prove it.
accepted_findings.position: Once per year, 30 days' notice, independent auditor, full access to usage and billing records, Lattice pays audit cost if overcharge exceeds 5%.
accepted_findings.fallback: Once per year, 45 days' notice, Lattice-approved auditor from Big Four accounting firms, limited to billing and usage records only, Lattice pays audit cost if overcharge exceeds 10%.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.7 Audit Rights. No more than once per calendar year, upon thirty (30) days' prior written notice, Customer may audit or cause an independent third-party auditor to audit Lattice's books and records relating to the calculation of Fees, usage metrics, and service-level performance under this Agreement. Such audit will be conducted during Lattice's normal business hours and in a manner that does not unreasonably interfere with Lattice's operations. The auditor must be bound by confidentiality obligations no less protective than those in Section 7. Customer will bear the cost of the audit unless the audit reveals an overpayment of more than five percent (5%) for the audited period, in which case Lattice will reimburse Customer for the reasonable cost of the audit and refund the overpayment within thirty (30) days.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with this specific contract's payment structure (annual advance payment) and connects audit rights to the actual risk of unverifiable usage-based charges at scale, though stops short of weighing whether to push given the high leverage posture.

accepted_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The liability cap in Section 8.6 contains no carve-out for gross negligence, willful misconduct, or fraud. Delaware courts generally enforce contractual liability caps even for gross negligence absent fraud or intentional misconduct, but commercial practice in technology contracts is to exclude such conduct from caps to preserve accountability for egregious acts. This is particularly important where the cap may be very low relative to potential harm. Recommend adding gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud as carved-out categories not subject to the cap.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice grossly mishandles Customer Data (e.g., intentionally disables encryption, ignores known critical vulnerabilities) and causes a data breach resulting in $5M in Customer damages, but the trailing-12-month fees are only $50K because this is a pilot engagement ramping to production, the cap limits Lattice's liability to $50K. While Delaware law may enforce this, it creates reputational risk and makes the contract commercially unreasonable. If Customer engages in willful misconduct (e.g., uses the Subscription Services to launch cyberattacks), Lattice's remedies are capped at trailing fees, which may be inadequate to cover Lattice's own third-party liability or regulatory exposure.
accepted_findings.position: Carve out gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud from the liability cap; such conduct is not subject to any cap.
accepted_findings.fallback: Carve out willful misconduct and fraud only; gross negligence remains subject to the cap.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's fee structure ($50K trailing fees vs. $5M damages), creates concrete scenarios tied to defined terms (Customer Data, Subscription Services), and analyzes how the cap interacts with pilot-to-production posture, though stops short of explicitly weighing whether to push given stated high leverage.

accepted_findings.source_text: If the Subscription Services become, or in Lattice's opinion are likely to become, the subject of an Infringement Claim, Lattice may, at its option and expense, (i) procure for Customer the right to continue using the Subscription Services, (ii) modify the Subscription Services so they no longer infringe, or (iii) terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: If the Subscription Services become, or in Lattice's opinion are likely to become, the subject of an Infringement Claim, Lattice will, at its option and expense, (i) procure for Customer the right to continue using the Subscription Services, or (ii) modify the Subscription Services so they no longer infringe without materially diminishing the functionality described in the applicable Order Form. If Lattice determines in its reasonable judgment that neither remedy (i) nor remedy (ii) can be accomplished on commercially reasonable terms, Lattice may (iii) terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination, provided that Lattice provides Customer with at least sixty (60) days' prior written notice and reasonable assistance in transitioning to an alternative solution.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 8.3 gives Lattice three remedy options when an Infringement Claim arises, including unilateral termination of the Subscription Services with a refund. While termination-and-refund is a standard backstop remedy, the current language permits Lattice to elect this remedy even when procuring continued-use rights or non-infringing modifications are feasible. This creates business-continuity risk for Customer, particularly if Customer has integrated the Subscription Services into critical operations. Recommend clarifying the hierarchy: Lattice must first attempt to procure rights or modify, and may terminate only when those options are not commercially reasonable. Also recommend a notice period and transition assistance obligation to mitigate operational disruption.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If a non-practicing entity asserts an Infringement Claim with uncertain merit, Lattice may find it cheaper to terminate the Subscription Services and refund fees than to procure a license or re-engineer. But if Customer relies on the Subscription Services for revenue-generating operations, sudden termination could cause Customer to breach its own downstream commitments, lose customers, or incur costs far exceeding the refund. The current language gives Lattice unfettered discretion to choose the cheapest remedy for Lattice regardless of impact on Customer. Requiring Lattice to exhaust the other remedies first, and providing a transition period, aligns with the commercial expectation that Subscription Services will remain available absent extraordinary circumstances.
accepted_findings.position: Lattice must attempt to procure rights or modify before terminating; termination requires 60 days' notice and transition assistance.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice must attempt to procure rights or modify; termination permitted if those options are not commercially reasonable, with 30 days' notice.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: If the Subscription Services become, or in Lattice's opinion are likely to become, the subject of an Infringement Claim, Lattice will, at its option and expense, (i) procure for Customer the right to continue using the Subscription Services, or (ii) modify the Subscription Services so they no longer infringe without materially diminishing the functionality described in the applicable Order Form. If Lattice determines in its reasonable judgment that neither remedy (i) nor remedy (ii) can be accomplished on commercially reasonable terms, Lattice may (iii) terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination, provided that Lattice provides Customer with at least sixty (60) days' prior written notice and reasonable assistance in transitioning to an alternative solution.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (subscription services model, remedy hierarchy, refund mechanism) and articulates concrete operational risks tied to Customer's reliance, though stops short of weighing deal leverage or providing specific redline recommendations.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: (c) Lattice shall name Customer as an additional insured on the commercial general liability policy with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's performance under this Agreement. Such additional insured coverage shall be provided on a primary and non-contributory basis with respect to Customer's own insurance. Lattice shall provide Customer with a certificate of insurance and additional insured endorsement evidencing such coverage prior to the Effective Date and upon each policy renewal.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Additional insured status on the CGL policy provides Customer with direct coverage for third-party bodily injury and property damage claims arising from Lattice's operations, which is standard for commercial service arrangements of this nature and value.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without additional insured status, Customer must rely solely on Lattice's indemnity obligation under Section 8.3 (limited to IP claims) and general breach remedies. If a third party brings a premises or operations claim against Customer arising from Lattice's conduct, Customer has no direct insurance recovery and must pursue Lattice contractually—introducing collection risk and delay.
accepted_findings.position: Customer is named as additional insured on Lattice's CGL policy for claims arising from Lattice's performance, with coverage primary and non-contributory to Customer's own insurance. Certificate and endorsement delivered before services commence and upon each renewal.
accepted_findings.fallback: Additional insured status on CGL only, without primary/non-contributory requirement, but with proactive certificate and endorsement delivery.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: (c) Lattice shall name Customer as an additional insured on the commercial general liability policy with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's performance under this Agreement. Such additional insured coverage shall be provided on a primary and non-contributory basis with respect to Customer's own insurance. Lattice shall provide Customer with a certificate of insurance and additional insured endorsement evidencing such coverage prior to the Effective Date and upon each policy renewal.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with the specific contract by cross-referencing Section 8.3's limitation to IP claims and explaining how the absence of additional insured status creates a gap in this particular agreement's risk allocation structure.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice's obligation to maintain the insurance required under this Section 9.2 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years, or, with respect to claims-made policies, Lattice shall maintain tail coverage for such three-year period.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Many claims arising from services performed during the Subscription Term are not reported until after termination. Post-termination insurance coverage ensures that claims arising from Lattice's prior performance remain insured and that Customer retains protection through the statute-of-limitations period.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice discontinues E&O or cyber coverage immediately upon termination, claims arising from incidents during the Subscription Term but reported post-termination are uninsured (because claims-made policies require both occurrence and reporting during the policy period). Customer's indemnity rights under Section 8.3 become collection claims against an uninsured Lattice, creating financial risk if the claim exceeds Lattice's uninsured assets.
accepted_findings.position: Lattice maintains all required insurance for three years post-termination, or provides equivalent extended reporting period (tail) coverage for claims-made policies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Two-year post-termination survival or tail coverage, with Customer having the option to purchase extended tail at its own expense if longer coverage is desired.
accepted_findings.walkaway: No post-termination insurance obligation for claims-made policies (E&O, cyber), leaving claims arising from in-term conduct but reported post-term uninsured.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice's obligation to maintain the insurance required under this Section 9.2 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years, or, with respect to claims-made policies, Lattice shall maintain tail coverage for such three-year period.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with the specific contract structure by referencing Section 8.3 indemnity rights and explaining how the claims-made policy mechanics interact with this agreement's terms to create a gap in coverage that affects the customer's practical remedy.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.5: 'Each SOW will identify the scope of work, deliverables (if any), fees, milestones, and any acceptance criteria.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Each SOW will identify the scope of work, deliverables, fees, milestones, and objective acceptance criteria. If an SOW does not specify acceptance criteria for a deliverable, such deliverable shall be deemed accepted if Customer does not provide written notice of non-conformance with the applicable SOW specifications within ten (10) business days of delivery.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current language makes deliverables and acceptance criteria optional, which creates commercial uncertainty in Professional Services engagements. Customer requires: elimination of the '(if any)' qualifier for deliverables (if there are no deliverables, that SOW is not subject to this section), and a default deemed-acceptance mechanism to prevent indefinite limbo where Customer neither accepts nor rejects work product.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without mandatory acceptance criteria and a deemed-acceptance fallback, Provider cannot close out Professional Services engagements or invoice final milestones when Customer fails to respond. This exposes Provider to indefinite liability for rework and prevents revenue recognition. In a services-heavy deal this creates material commercial exposure.
accepted_findings.position: Replace clause (b) of Section 2.5 as shown in proposed_text above.
accepted_findings.fallback: Retain optional acceptance criteria but add deemed-acceptance language: 'If Customer does not provide written notice of non-conformance within [10] business days of delivery of any deliverable, such deliverable shall be deemed accepted.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Each SOW will identify the scope of work, deliverables, fees, milestones, and objective acceptance criteria. If an SOW does not specify acceptance criteria for a deliverable, such deliverable shall be deemed accepted if Customer does not provide written notice of non-conformance with the applicable SOW specifications within ten (10) business days of delivery.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (SOW-based professional services with milestones and invoicing) and articulates how the absence of acceptance criteria creates commercial exposure particular to this services arrangement, though stops short of partner-level trade-off analysis.

rejected_findings.proposed_text: 8.3A Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data, when used by Lattice in accordance with this Agreement, directly infringes such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A are subject to the indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5.
rejected_findings.external_comment: The Agreement obligates Lattice to indemnify Customer for third-party IP claims arising from the Subscription Services, but contains no reciprocal indemnification from Customer for third-party claims arising from Customer Data. Customer warrants it has all necessary rights in Customer Data, but provides no indemnity to backstop that warranty when claims arise from content Customer uploads.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: If a third party sues Lattice alleging that Customer Data infringes that party's IP rights, Lattice bears all defense costs and liability with no recourse against Customer beyond breach-of-warranty damages subject to the general cap. In a high-data-volume SaaS model, Customer Data represents the primary IP risk vector for the provider. Without reciprocal indemnity, Customer can breach Section 8.4 and leave Lattice exposed to uncapped third-party claims.
rejected_findings.position: Reciprocal third-party IP indemnity for claims arising from Customer Data, with same procedure and carve-outs as Section 8.3.
rejected_findings.fallback: Customer indemnity limited to third-party IP claims where Customer had actual knowledge of infringement at time of upload, or where Customer failed to obtain necessary licenses after written notice from Lattice.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: 8.3A Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data, when used by Lattice in accordance with this Agreement, directly infringes such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A are subject to the indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 carves out confidentiality breaches from liability cap (unlimited exposure) + Section 7.1 defines Customer Data as Lattice's Confidential Information + Section 5.3 Security Incident notification obligation with 72-hour window + Section 8.2 disclaimer 'DOES NOT WARRANT THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED'
auditor_findings.external_comment: CROSS-SECTION HAZARD: The interaction of four provisions creates existential unlimited liability that no individual specialist would flag in isolation. Section 8.6(B) creates unlimited liability for confidentiality breach. Section 7.1 defines Customer Data as Lattice Confidential Information. Section 5.3 requires Security Incident notification within 72 hours. Section 8.2 disclaims any warranty that Customer Data will be secure. Result: Any data breach triggers both Security Incident reporting (Section 5.3) AND unlimited confidentiality-breach liability (Section 8.6(B)), while the security disclaimer (Section 8.2) means Lattice has no contractual obligation to prevent the breach. Customer Data exposure becomes an unlimited-liability event with no corresponding security obligation. For a SaaS provider processing customer data at scale, this is an existential trap: one breach = unlimited damages, but no contractual security standard beyond the process obligations in Section 5.2.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Risk-allocation-analyst correctly flagged unlimited confidentiality liability (finding 002) and performance-obligations-analyst correctly flagged the security disclaimer contradiction (finding 003), but neither specialist saw the cross-section effect: the security disclaimer + unlimited confidentiality liability + Customer Data definition creates a structure where Lattice has unlimited exposure for an event it has disclaimed any obligation to prevent. The E&O/cyber insurance in Section 9.2 ($5M) is nine figures short of typical data-breach exposure in class actions. Customer faces the mirror risk: paying for a service with no enforceable security warranty but unlimited vendor liability that will bankrupt the vendor on first breach, eliminating Customer's recovery. This is an existential hazard for both parties that emerges only from reading all four sections together.
auditor_findings.position: Either (1) cap confidentiality liability at 3x-5x the general cap per risk-allocation-analyst-002, OR (2) remove the security disclaimer from Section 8.2 and make security obligations expressly warranted, OR (3) narrow Section 7.1 so that Customer Data is Customer's Confidential Information (not Lattice's), eliminating the unlimited-liability trigger on breach.
auditor_findings.fallback: Accept 5x super-cap for confidentiality breach AND remove security/data-loss disclaimer from Section 8.2, creating bounded liability with enforceable security obligations.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Unlimited confidentiality liability + security disclaimer + Customer Data defined as Lattice Confidential Information. This structure is uninsurable and creates bankruptcy risk on first incident.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Unlimited liability carve-outs are enforceable, but the interaction with warranty disclaimers has not been tested in Delaware courts in this specific configuration. Insurance coverage for contractual liability this broad is typically excluded under standard cyber policies.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b) payment acceleration (Customer pays 100% of remaining contract value on termination) + Section 4.2 auto-renewal with 60-day notice requirement + Section 3.5 uncapped price increases at each renewal + Section 4.3 asymmetric 30/60-day cure periods
auditor_findings.external_comment: CROSS-SECTION HAZARD: The termination and renewal provisions combine to create a compounding lock-in with escalating exit costs that no single specialist would flag as existential. Customer faces: (1) auto-renewal every 12 months unless 60-day advance notice given; (2) uncapped price increases at each renewal, taking effect automatically if Customer misses the 60-day window; (3) 100% payment of remaining contract value if Customer terminates for convenience at any point; (4) only 30 days to cure breaches before Lattice can terminate and trigger the payment acceleration, versus 60 days for Customer to terminate Lattice. Result: Customer who misses one 60-day non-renewal notice is locked into another year at whatever price Lattice sets, and if Customer attempts to exit mid-term, Customer owes 100% of the inflated renewal fees for the full year. The asymmetric cure periods mean Lattice can terminate Customer (and collect acceleration) twice as fast as Customer can terminate Lattice. Over a multi-year relationship with compounding renewals, this creates exponential lock-in: Year 1 at $100K, Year 2 auto-renews at $130K (30% increase), Customer is now locked in for $130K with $130K exit cost; Year 3 auto-renews at $169K, exit cost is now $169K. Commercial-terms-analyst flagged each piece individually but the existential character emerges from the combination.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Commercial-terms-analyst correctly identified payment acceleration as blocker/existential (finding 001), price increases as moderate (finding 004), and termination-remedies-analyst identified cure asymmetry as blocker/existential (finding 001), but the cross-section hazard is that these provisions compound: uncapped renewal increases + auto-renewal + 100% acceleration + asymmetric cure creates a ratchet where Customer's exit costs escalate automatically every 12 months while Customer's ability to force Lattice's exit is structurally delayed. In a high-leverage their-paper scenario, this is the vendor's core lock-in mechanism and it operates across four sections that individual specialists treated separately. The existential element is the compounding effect: not just that exit is expensive, but that the expense escalates automatically and Customer has no mechanism to cap it short of perfect calendar discipline on 60-day notices.
auditor_findings.position: Require ALL of: (1) cap on payment acceleration per commercial-terms-analyst-001 (50% with 6-month ceiling); (2) cap on price increases per commercial-terms-analyst-004 (5% or CPI); (3) mutual 60-day cure periods per termination-remedies-analyst-001; (4) termination-for-convenience right with capped fee after Year 1.
auditor_findings.fallback: Accept payment acceleration at 75% with 12-month cap AND price increase cap at 7% or CPI AND mutual 60-day cure. This breaks the compounding ratchet by capping both the exit cost and the annual escalation.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Any structure that combines uncapped price increases + 100% payment acceleration + auto-renewal. Customer cannot budget or exit under this structure, making the relationship existentially unmanageable.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Auto-renewal + price escalation + payment acceleration clauses are individually enforceable, but the compounding effect may trigger unconscionability analysis if the exit costs become punitive. Delaware courts have not addressed this specific combination in published SaaS decisions.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 9.1 permits unlimited subcontracting without notice or consent + Section 5.2 information security obligations + Section 9.2 insurance requirements do not flow down to subcontractors + Section 8.3 IP indemnity carve-outs include 'use in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Lattice'
auditor_findings.external_comment: CROSS-SECTION HAZARD: Section 9.1 allows Lattice to subcontract any obligation, including Customer Data processing, to any third party without notice to Customer. Section 5.2 obligates Lattice to maintain security safeguards for Customer Data. Section 9.2 requires Lattice to maintain insurance, but contains no flow-down requirement for subcontractors. Section 8.3 carves out IP indemnity if the infringement arises from 'use in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Lattice.' Result: Lattice can route Customer Data through subcontractors with no security vetting visibility to Customer, no insurance requirement on the subcontractor, and if the subcontractor's platform infringes IP, Section 8.3 may eliminate Lattice's indemnity obligation because the infringement arose from the subcontractor's product (not provided by Lattice). Customer has no line of sight into the subcontractor supply chain, no contractual privity with subcontractors, and potentially no indemnity coverage for subcontractor-originated claims.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Individual specialists did not flag this because: insurance-coverage-analyst reviewed Section 9.2 in isolation and noted no subcontractor insurance requirements but did not cross-reference Section 9.1's unlimited subcontracting right; risk-allocation-analyst reviewed Section 8.3 indemnity carve-outs and noted the 'combination with non-Lattice products' language but did not connect it to subcontractors; protective-provisions-analyst reviewed data handling but Section 5.2 does not explicitly address subcontractor security obligations. The hazard emerges when Customer Data flows to a subcontractor: Customer loses security visibility (no notice under Section 9.1), loses insurance backstop (no subcontractor insurance requirement under Section 9.2), and may lose indemnity coverage (Section 8.3 carve-out for non-Lattice products). For a SaaS platform relying on third-party infrastructure (cloud hosting, CDN, payment processors), this is a material gap.
auditor_findings.position: Require: (1) advance written notice of subcontractors processing Customer Data, with right to object to subcontractors that do not meet equivalent security standards; (2) flow-down of insurance requirements to subcontractors handling Customer Data or providing core platform functionality; (3) clarification that Section 8.3 indemnity applies to infringement arising from Lattice-authorized subcontractors.
auditor_findings.fallback: Require annual list of material subcontractors with right to audit subcontractor security upon reasonable notice, and clarify that 'Subscription Services' in Section 8.3 includes subcontractor-provided components authorized by Lattice.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Subcontracting rights and indemnity carve-outs are interpreted according to their terms; no implied limitation on subcontracting in Delaware commercial law absent express restriction.

auditor_findings.proposed_text: 2.6 Service Modifications and Discontinuation. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ninety (90) days' advance written notice before discontinuing any material feature or functionality of the Subscription Services, or before making any modification that materially diminishes the core functionality described in the applicable Order Form. If Lattice discontinues a material feature or functionality and cannot provide a commercially reasonable substitute, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form upon thirty (30) days' written notice and receive a pro-rated refund of prepaid Fees for the terminated portion of the Subscription Term.
auditor_findings.external_comment: MATERIAL OMISSION: No specialist flagged the absence of service-discontinuation or material-modification obligations, and no specialist's coverage_pass marked this as present, cross-referenced, or not-applicable. Section 2.4 permits Lattice to 'modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time' with only a prohibition on modifications that 'materially diminish the core functionality... during the then-current Subscription Term.' This language permits Lattice to discontinue features entirely at renewal, or to make breaking changes mid-term as long as Lattice characterizes them as not materially diminishing core functionality (a subjective standard with no enforcement mechanism). For a customer relying on specific features for business operations, this creates a risk that the service Customer contracted for is unilaterally changed or eliminated with no notice, no refund, and no exit right.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Performance-obligations-analyst's coverage_pass marked scheduled maintenance as cross-referenced to Exhibit A, but maintenance windows are distinct from feature discontinuation. No specialist reviewed feature-deprecation or service-modification notice obligations because no provision in the four corners addresses this beyond Section 2.4's 'may modify' language. The omission is material because: (1) Customer pays annually in advance (Section 3.2) for a service that can be materially changed or discontinued; (2) the subscription model creates dependency—Customer integrates the platform into operations and cannot easily switch; (3) SaaS providers routinely deprecate features, sunset APIs, and force migrations to new versions, often with inadequate notice. Without a contractual notice and exit-right provision, Customer has no remedy when critical features are discontinued mid-term or at renewal. The specialist structure meant no single specialist had this in their domain: performance-obligations-analyst looked at SLAs and warranties, not feature lifecycle; commercial-terms-analyst looked at pricing and payment, not service composition.
auditor_findings.position: Ninety-day advance notice for material feature discontinuation or modification, with termination right and pro-rated refund if no commercially reasonable substitute is provided.
auditor_findings.fallback: Sixty-day advance notice, with termination right and refund only if the discontinued feature was explicitly identified in the Order Form as core functionality.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Service modification rights in SaaS agreements are governed by contract terms; Delaware courts apply reasonable-expectations doctrine but require express contractual language for customer protection against unilateral service changes.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: 2.6 Service Modifications and Discontinuation. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ninety (90) days' advance written notice before discontinuing any material feature or functionality of the Subscription Services, or before making any modification that materially diminishes the core functionality described in the applicable Order Form. If Lattice discontinues a material feature or functionality and cannot provide a commercially reasonable substitute, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form upon thirty (30) days' written notice and receive a pro-rated refund of prepaid Fees for the terminated portion of the Subscription Term.

§3.3: 'Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower'

§3.4: 'If any amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.'
No provision addresses Customer's right to withhold disputed amounts or procedures for invoice disputes.

§3.1: 'Fees do not include any taxes, levies, duties, or similar governmental assessments of any nature, including value-added, sales, use, or withholding taxes ("Taxes"), and Customer is responsible for payment of all Taxes associated with its purchases hereunder, excluding only Taxes based on Lattice's net income.'
No provision grants Customer set-off or recoupment rights against amounts owed to Lattice.
No back-charge or chargeback provisions present; not typical for SaaS subscription agreements.
No MFC or price-protection clause present in the contract.

§3.5: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.'
No audit or inspection rights granted to Customer to verify invoicing accuracy, usage metrics, or compliance with service levels.
§3.2 specifies annual invoicing in advance and payment terms, but does not detail invoice format, required documentation, or itemization requirements.
No pay-when-paid or pay-if-paid provisions (appropriate for SaaS contract).
§3.2: 'All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars' establishes currency but does not address FX risk allocation if Customer operates in other currencies.

No separate cap for data-breach or Security Incident liability; confidentiality breach (Section 7) is carved out of the cap per Section 8.6(B) but with no stated super-cap amount

Section 8.6 and 8.7 contain no carve-outs for gross negligence or willful misconduct; cap and consequential waiver apply to all conduct

Section 10.2: 'THE PARTIES KNOWINGLY AND VOLUNTARILY WAIVE ANY RIGHT TO A TRIAL BY JURY.'
Section 10.2: 'THE PARTIES KNOWINGLY AND VOLUNTARILY WAIVE...ANY RIGHT TO PARTICIPATE IN A CLASS, COLLECTIVE, OR REPRESENTATIVE ACTION.'
Section 10.2: 'American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules...in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator...Each Party will bear its own costs and fees.'
Section 9.3: defines Force Majeure Events, obligations during event, and 60-day termination trigger.

Section 7.1 defines Confidential Information as 'any non-public information disclosed by one Party (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party (the "Receiving Party"), whether orally, in writing, or in any other form, that is designated as confidential or that, given the nature of the information and the circumstances of disclosure, would reasonably be understood to be confidential.'

Section 7.3 includes exceptions for: (a) already known without restriction; (b) publicly available through no breach; (c) independently developed; (d) rightfully received from third party. Also includes compelled disclosure exception with notice requirement.

Section 7.4: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.'
Section 7.2(a): 'to use Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party only for purposes of performing its obligations or exercising its rights under this Agreement'

coverage_pass_aggregate.item: Standard of care for Confidential Information protection
coverage_pass_aggregate.evidence: Section 7.2(b): 'to protect Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party using at least the same degree of care it uses to protect its own confidential information of similar nature, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care'

Section 7.2(c): 'to limit access to Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to those employees, contractors, and agents who have a need to know such information and who are bound by confidentiality obligations no less protective than those set forth herein.'

Section 6.1: 'Lattice and its licensors own and shall retain all right, title, and interest in and to the Subscription Services, the Documentation, and any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on any of the foregoing'
Section 6.2: 'As between the Parties, Customer retains all right, title, and interest in and to Customer Data, subject to the license granted to Lattice in Section 5.1.'

coverage_pass_aggregate.item: Work made for hire language scope
coverage_pass_aggregate.evidence: No 'work made for hire' language appears in the contract.

coverage_pass_aggregate.item: License grant to Customer - scope and limitations
coverage_pass_aggregate.evidence: Section 2.1: 'Lattice grants Customer a non-exclusive, non-transferable, non-sublicensable right during the Subscription Term to access and use the Subscription Services solely for Customer's internal business purposes'

coverage_pass_aggregate.item: License grant to Provider - Customer Data
coverage_pass_aggregate.evidence: Section 5.1: 'Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, worldwide, royalty-free license to host, copy, transmit, display, and otherwise process Customer Data solely as necessary for Lattice to provide and support the Subscription Services in accordance with this Agreement.'

coverage_pass_aggregate.item: Deliverables and work product ownership - Professional Services
coverage_pass_aggregate.evidence: Section 2.5: 'Lattice retains all right, title, and interest in any methodology, know-how, tools, and pre-existing materials used in connection with Professional Services.' No express ownership allocation for deliverables created under SOWs.
No non-compete provision appears in the contract.
No non-solicitation provision appears in the contract.
No non-disparagement provision appears in the contract.
No publicity, marketing rights, or use-of-name/logo provision appears in the contract.
No residuals clause or treatment of information retained in memory appears in the contract.

Section 6.1 states Lattice retains 'all improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on any of the foregoing' without addressing joint contributions or Customer-funded development.
